coredata.dc:title: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as the chemiluminescence probe for detection of Cu2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Manzoori, Jamshid L.
Hallaj, Tooba
Azizi, Neda
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we investigated the direct chemiluminescence (CL) of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) induced by some common oxidants. The results showed that KMnO4 can produce much more intensive CL than other oxidants. The mechanism of the CL reaction was proposed based on the UV–vis, fluorescence and CL emission spectra. Moreover, the effect of some metal ions on the S,N-CQDs–KMnO4 CL system was studied and found that the CL intensity of this system was significantly declined by Cu2+ ions. Based on this effect, a simple and selective CL sensor was established for the detection of trace amount of Cu2+ in the concentration range of 0.01–0.5mgL−1 with a limit of detection (3s) of 2.1µgL−1. The method was exploited for the determination of Cu2+ in human plasma and water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
Carbon nanomaterial
Chemiluminescence
Copper

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of some metal ions on CL reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) FT-IR spectrum and (c) EDS spectrum of S,N-CQDs.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum of S,N-CQDs, (b) Fluorescence spectra for S,N-CQDs excited at wavelengths of 355, 365, 375, 385, 400, 425 and 450nm, respectively (a–g).

The CL intensity of various amounts of S,N-CQDs with different oxidants. Optimum conditions: KMnO4, 7.5×10−6 molL−1, HCl, 0.2molL−1; K3Fe(CN)6, 1.5×10−3, NaOH, 1.5molL−1; H2O2, 5×10−2 molL−1, NaOH, 5×10−2 molL−1; KIO4, 3×10−3 molL−1, NaOH, 5×10−4 molL−1.
Kinetic curve for S,N-CQDs/KMnO4 CL system (a) in the absence and (b) in the presence of 0.1mgL−1 Cu2+. Conditions: S,N-CQDs, 150µL, KMnO4, 10−5 molL−1, H3PO4, 0.4M.

(a) CL spectrum of S,N-CQDs-KMnO4 system, obtained with continuous flow of reagents: S,N-CQDs solution and H3PO4 (0.8M) in one line and KMnO4 (2×10−5 M) in other line; (b) fluorescence spectrum of S,N-CQDs at λex=425nm.
Effect of different metal ions (2.0mgL−1) on the CL intensity of S,N-CQDs-KMnO4.
Determination of Cu2+ in plasma and water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit for added and found amounts is (mgL−1) for samples.

head.ce:title: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as the chemiluminescence probe for detection of Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: aDepartment of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: bDepartment of Chemistry, Tabriz Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Islamic Azad University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we investigated the direct chemiluminescence (CL) of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) induced by some common oxidants. The results showed that KMnO4 can produce much more intensive CL than other oxidants. The mechanism of the CL reaction was proposed based on the UV–vis, fluorescence and CL emission spectra. Moreover, the effect of some metal ions on the S,N-CQDs–KMnO4 CL system was studied and found that the CL intensity of this system was significantly declined by Cu2+ ions. Based on this effect, a simple and selective CL sensor was established for the detection of trace amount of Cu2+ in the concentration range of 0.01–0.5mgL−1 with a limit of detection (3s) of 2.1µgL−1. The method was exploited for the determination of Cu2+ in human plasma and water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon nanomaterials such as carbon and graphene quantum dots (CQDs and GQDs) have inspired substantial studies in different areas because of the alluring physicochemical properties. They are typically carbonaceous nanoparticles containing amorphous to nanocrystalline core with an outer shell composed of oxygen containing functional groups [1–3]. Nowadays, many attempts are made to dope CQDs and GQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), sulfur (S), phosphorous (P) and fluorine (F) in order to achieve QDs with versatile properties and extend their application [4]. One of the reported heteroatom-doped CQDs, which are extremely attractive because of its high quantum yield, is sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs). They have been used in various fields such as bioimaging [5–7], sensing [8–12] photocatalysis [13] and electrochemical catalysis [14].

Advantages such as high sensitivity, simplicity of instrumentation and short analysis time have made the chemiluminescence (CL) one of the attractive analytical tools. Various luminescent carbon nanomaterials have been applied in CL reactions as catalyst [15–18], luminophore or energy acceptor [19–26]. Direct CL of some carbon nanomaterials has also been studied by using oxidants such as Ce(IV) [27,28], K3Fe(CN)6 [29–31], hypochlorite [32,33] and KMnO4 [27,34]. Our previous works [28,29] have shown that CL properties of carbon nanomaterials greatly depend on their structure and synthesis procedure. Accordingly it is expected that the CL behavior of S,N-CQDs is different from other carbon nanomaterials and so the study of this behavior is important both from fundamental and analytical point of views. To the best of our knowledge, such study for S,N-CQDs has not yet been performed.

In this contribution, the direct CL of S,N-CQDs induced by some common oxidants was systematically studied. The S,N-CQDs–KMnO4 reaction was introduced as a new CL system and its possible mechanism was investigated. Furthermore, based on the diminishing effect of Cu2+ ions on this CL system, a simple and selective method was established for the determination of trace amount of Cu2+ in human plasma and water samples. Copper is one of essential trace elements in living systems which requires for structural and catalytic properties of important enzymes. However, it can be harmful to human health in high concentrations due to damaging to liver, renal tubules, the brain, and other organs [35]. Therefore, the analysis of trace concentration of copper in environmental and biological samples is of great importance.

The CL signals were measured by LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold; www.berthold.com). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by RF-540 spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan). CL spectrum was recorded by the same instrument using flow mode with the excitation light source being turned off. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Cary-100 Spectrophotometer (Varian; www.varianinc.com). The size and shape of S,N-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Bruker).

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co. Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment. Citric acid monohydrate, l-cysteine, NaOH, KMnO4, Cu(NO3)2 and phosphoric acid were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Human plasma samples were obtained from Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran).

S,N-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method according to the literature with some modifications [36]. Briefly, 2.0g of citric acid and 1.0g of l-cysteine were mixed and grounded to a uniform powder. The solid mixture was transferred into a 100mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 3h in a muffle furnace. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the pH of resultant thick syrup was adjusted to 7 with 1M NaOH, and the volume of solution was reached to 50mL with deionized water and stored at 4°C. The working solution was obtained by 25-fold dilution of stock solution with deionized water.

CL measurements were performed in a 3mL tube (with 75 mm height and 12 mm diameter) in the static injection mode. In a typical experiment, 150μL of diluted S,N-CQDs and 100μL of H3PO4 (4M) were added into the cell. Then an appropriate volume of sample or standard solution was added and the volume was reached to 800μL with deionized water. Finally, the CL reaction was triggered by automatic injection of 200μL of 5×10−5 M KMnO4. At the same time, the CL intensity of reaction was automatically measured versus time and the maximum value of CL intensity was recorded as the analytical signal.

A 500-µL aliquot of plasma was placed in a centrifuge tube and 500µL of 10% trichloroacetic acid was added to precipitate proteins. The resulting mixture was centrifuged for 15min. Then the supernatant solution was transferred into another centrifuge tube and 2.0mL of 0.1M Ba(OH)2 and 1.8mL of 0.1M ZnSO4 were added for further precipitating the remaining proteins. The obtained mixture was centrifuged for another 15min. At last, the supernatant solution was transferred into a 5.0-mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with water. An appropriate portion of this solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.

The TEM image of S,N-CQDs ( Fig. 1a) synthesized by hydrothermal method shows that they are of spherical shape and nearly monodisperse nanoparticles with size distribution in the range of 10–15nm. FT-IR spectrum of S,N-CQDs is shown in (Fig. 1b). The peaks at 1639 and 1711cm−1 are attributed to C=C and C=O stretching vibrations, respectively and the peak at about 1398cm−1 to the stretching vibration of C-N. The peaks at about 1221 and 2596cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C=S and S–H, respectively. Moreover, the broad band in 3100cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. EDS spectrum (Fig. 1c) indicates that the nitrogen and sulfur contents of S,N-CQDs are about 9.5 and 7.8 w%, respectively. These results confirmed the doping of N and S atoms in the prepared CQDs.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of S,N-CQDs ( Fig. 2a) exhibits the absorption peaks attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C and n–π* transition of C=O at ∼210 and ∼350nm, respectively. The fluorescence behavior of S,N-CQDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 2b, when the excitation wavelength changes from 355 to 385nm, the fluorescence spectra are almost invariable. But for excitation wavelengths above 385nm, the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs shifts to higher wavelengths. The maximum emission of 430nm was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 375nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared S,N-CQDs was found to be about 82% by using quinine sulfate as a standard.

Firstly, we examined some commonly used oxidants in CL reactions (including K3Fe(CN)6, KMnO4, H2O2, and NaIO4) to elicit the CL from S,N-CQD solution. All of these oxidants were more or less able to directly elicit a CL from S,N-CQDs. Then, the experimental conditions were optimized for each of these oxidants and under the respective optimum conditions (given in caption to Fig. 3) the reactions between them and various concentrations of S,N-CQDs were examined. As can be seen in Fig. 3, compared to the other oxidants, KMnO4 could elicit much more intensive CL emission from S,N-CQDs. Therefore, we selected KMnO4 as the best oxidant for the CL reaction. A control experiment with the reagents used for the preparation of S,N-CQDs (the mixture of citric acid and cysteine) was also carried out. Very weak CL was found for this solution, which confirms that the CL arises from S,N-CQDs. The kinetic profile of S,N-CQDs –KMnO4 CL system ( Fig. 4) showed that the CL reaction was very fast and the CL intensity reached a maximum value within 1s after injection of the oxidant solution.

In order to study the CL reaction mechanism and determine the emitting species, following experiments were performed. At first, the CL emission spectrum of S,N-CQDs –KMnO4 system was recorded by a spectrofluorimeter in flow mode. In the obtained spectrum ( Fig. 5a) there was an emission band with a maximum at ∼500nm, which is similar to the S,N-CQDs fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5b). Therefore the CL can be assigned to the excited S,N-CQDs. Furthermore, the effect of KMnO4 on the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs was examined. We observed that after mixing S,N-CQD solution with this oxidant the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs decreased (Fig. S1, Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM). The UV–vis absorption spectra of KMnO4 were also recorded in the presence and absence of S,N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S2 (ESM), in presence of S,N-CQDs, the absorption of KMnO4 decreased with the reaction time. So it can be concluded that a chemical reaction occurred between S,N-CQDs and KMnO4. Based on these facts, the CL reaction can be described in its simplest form as follows:
S,N-CQD+KMnO4→S,N-CQDs*+Mn(II)
S,N-CQDs*→S,N-CQDs+hν (∼500nm)

As suggested in previous works, the CL reaction of CQDs involves the formation of excited-state CQDs (CQDs*) through electron-transfer annihilation. Oxidants, such as KMnO4, can inject holes into the CQDs. Then, the injected holes can interact with available electrons in the high energy band of CQDs. Indeed, the injection of holes into the CQDs by oxidants increases the holes in the CQDs and accelerates the electrons–hole annihilation, which results in energy release in the form of CL emission [27,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of some metal ions on CL reaction
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the analytical applicability of the S,N-CQDs –KMnO4 CL system, the effect of metal ions including Ag+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cr(VI), Al3+, Au3+ and Eu3+ on the system was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 6, compared to the other metal ions that have negligible effects on the CL intensity, Cu2+ ions could significantly diminish the CL intensity of S,N-CQDs-KMnO4 system. This effect can be attributed to interaction between Cu2+ and S,N-CQDs. Due to presence some groups with reducing activity on the surface of CQDs, they could serve as the electron donor in the reaction with electron-withdrawing agents. Since Cu2+ is a paramagnetic ion with an unfilled d shell, it is possible to act as electron acceptors and an electron transfer process can take place between them and S,N-CQDs [37,38]. The fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs which decreased in the presence of Cu2+ also confirmed the interaction between them (Fig. S3, ESM). Based on this fact, we designed a new selective CL sensor for the determination of Cu2+.

In order to obtain the maximum sensitivity for the determination of Cu2+, the effect of several variables such as concentration of acid, permanganate and S,N-CQDs on the CL response (ΔI=I0−I, where I0 and I are the CL intensities in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively) was studied.

One of the significant parameters which influences on the intensity of permanganate-based CL systems is kind of acid. We examined the effects of several acids (including HCl, H2SO4 and H3PO4) with different concentrations on CL response. As can be seen in Fig. S4a (ESM) the maximum response was obtained for 0.4M H3PO4.

The effect of S,N-CQD amount on the response over the range of 10–500µL was examined (Fig. S4b, ESM). It was found that increasing S,N-CQD amount enhanced the CL response, however at higher amounts of S,N-CQDs the response decreased which is due to the excess amount of CQDs with respect to Cu2+ ions. Since the maximum response was obtained at 150µL of S,N-CQDs, this amount was used as the optimum amount for subsequent experiments.

Furthermore, the influence of permanganate concentration over the range of 1.25×10−6 to 1.5×10− 5 M was investigated. As shown in Fig. S4c, the CL response increased until 1.0×10−4 M permanganate and then decreased at higher concentrations probably because of CL quenching by excess KMnO4.

We designed a sensitive and selective sensor for the detection of Cu2+ based on diminishing effect of Cu2+ on the S,N-CQDs-KMnO4 CL intensity. Under the selected conditions described above, the analytical figures of merit for the determination of Cu2+ was obtained. The calibration curve was linear in the range of 0.01–0.5mgL−1 with a limit of detection (3s) of 2.1µgL−1 (Fig. S5, ESM). The regression equation was ΔI=10+6 C+116653, R2=0.9964, where ΔI=I0−I is the difference between the CL intensity in the presence of Cu2+ (I) and in its absence (I0), and C is concentration of Cu2+ in mg L−1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained to be 1.75% for five replicate determinations of 0.05mgL−1 Cu2+. Comparison between the proposed method and some other reported CL methods for the Cu2+ quantification is shown in Table S1 (ESM). As can be seen, this method has better limit of detection than most of other methods. Moreover, unlike other methods, which suffer from poor selectivity, the developed method has good selectivity (vide infra) and can be applied for analysis of complex samples such as biological fluids. It should be mentioned that, though the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs is also quenched by Cu2+ ions, but only high concentrations of Cu2+ (in mg L−1 range) can be detected by fluorescence and so the sensitivity is much lower than the CL method.

To demonstrate the selectivity of the introduced method, we tested the effects of some common species on the determination of 0.05mgL−1 Cu2+. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in relative error of <5% were over 1000 for Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Eu3+, As(V), Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+,Al3+, NO3 -, Cl-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, glucose, sucrose, lactose, glycine, 500 for Co2+, 100 for Hg2+, Au3+ and uric acid, and 10 for ascorbic acid and Fe2+. As can be seen, the amounts of most of the potentially interfering species are below their tolerable levels or can be decreased with diluting, so there would be no interferences from these species in Cu2+ determination.

The method was exploited to the determination of Cu2+ in human plasma and water samples. A two-step deproteinization described in experimental section was found to be necessary for plasma samples. The results are reported in Table 1. Furthermore, the obtained results for plasma samples were compared with those achieved by atomic absorption spectrometry to confirm the accuracy of analysis.

In this work, direct CL of S,N-CQDs induced by some common oxidants was investigated and S,N-CQDs- KMnO4 CL reaction was introduced as a new CL system. The CL emission of this system can be attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and excited electrons in the S,N-CQDs. Furthermore, we found that the CL intensity of this system is significantly decreased by Cu2+ ions due to their interaction with S,N-CQDs. Based on this inhibiting effect, a simple and sensitive CL method was designed for the determination of trace amount of Cu2+ in human plasma and water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2016.10.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J.C.G. Esteves da Silva, H.M.R. Gonçalves, 30, 2011, pp. 1327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta. A. Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y. Wang, W. Wu, M. Wu, H. Sun, H. Xie, C. Hu, X. Wu, J. Qiu, 30, 2015, pp. 550.
S. Liu, J. Tian, L. Wang, Y. Zhang, X. Qin, Y. Luo, A.M. Asiri, A.O. Al-Youbi, X. Sun, 24, 2012, pp. 2037.
